results.possibleIdentity:
Thomas L. Wayburn (known as Tom Wayburn) is a multifaceted engineer, mathematician, and musician who was active in the New York City technical and artistic scenes during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in 1934 in Detroit, he earned a B.S. in Chemical and Metallurgical Engineering from the University of Michigan in 1956. He later moved to New York City, where he obtained an M.S. in Mathematics from the Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences (CIMS) at New York University (NYU) in 1968 and completed coursework for a Ph.D. in Mathematics by 1970. His technical career involved chemical process design and early software development for chemical engineering, specifically working with the CACHE (Computer Aids for Chemical Engineering) Corporation and programs like FLOWTRAN. He was an instructor and assistant professor at several institutions, including Clarkson University (1983–1987) and potentially City College of New York (CCNY), as noted in historical records from the period. 

Beyond his engineering work, Wayburn was a professional jazz drummer and recording engineer, notably associated with the 'Jazz Loft' scene at 821 Sixth Avenue in Manhattan during the late 1950s and 1960s. He recorded with legendary jazz pianist Lennie Tristano and was a contemporary of musicians like Philly Joe Jones and Chet Baker. His unique dual identity as an engineer and an artist aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded in 1966 to foster collaborations between the technical and creative communities. Wayburn's later work shifted toward political economy and energy analysis, where he developed theories on 'Dematerialism' and studied Energy Return on Energy Invested (ERoEI). He eventually earned a Ph.D. in Chemical Engineering from the University of Utah in 1983 and resided in Brooklyn and Manhattan before later moving to Houston, Texas.
